1:
"He that entereth not by the door into the sheepfold, but climbeth up some other way, the same is a thief and a robber." The witnesses read the opening of the Good Shepherd allegory as Christ's answer to the Pharisees who had cast out the healed blind man (John 9:34): he marks the true shepherd from the false, leaving them to see themselves.

The Eastern and Latin fathers supply the allegory's keys, with notable variety. Chrysostom: "the Scriptures he calls the door" — "he that uses not the Scriptures, but climbs up some other way, is a thief" — an allusion to false Christs and Antichrist; the porter "perhaps is Moses, for to him the oracles of God were committed." Theophylact makes the porter "the Holy Spirit, by whom the Scriptures are unlocked." Augustine reads the door as Christ himself, so that to enter is to "preach Christ, seek Christ's glory, not his own"; "Christ is a lowly door, and he who enters must be lowly" — and even ventures the porter is Christ, or the Spirit who "will guide you into all truth."

The Reformed and grammatical-historical readers apply it to ministry and to the sheep. Gill: the design is to show "that Christ is the true and only shepherd," while the Scribes and Pharisees "were thieves and robbers," usurping a rule not given them. Robertson: "a shepherd to the sheep" who enters by the legitimate door (1 Peter 2:25; Hebrews 13:20). Lightfoot details the Talmudic sheepfold — the pen where sheep were gathered, milked, and tithed.

The Puritan Henry frames the whole: the Pharisees held "that they were the pastors of the church, and that Jesus... was an intruder"; Christ describes "who were the false shepherds, and who the true, leaving them to infer." Augustine's deepest note settles the difficulty that wolves sometimes hear and sheep sometimes stray: "the Lord knows them that are his... the predestinated" (Luke 10:20; Ezekiel 34:4) — "many sheep are without the fold, many wolves within." So the witnesses agree: the lawful entrance is Christ and his word; all self-appointed authority over the flock is theft.
2:
"But he that entereth in by the door is the shepherd of the sheep." The witnesses read the positive mark of the true shepherd — lawful entrance and rightful care — and most apply it, in this verse, to the ministers who enter through Christ.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the reference. Barnes: here Christ "does not refer to himself, for he is the way or door by which others enter; but he refers to all the ministers of the gospel" who enter "in accordance with his spirit and law." Robertson notes the anarthrous "a shepherd to the sheep," who "comes in by the door with the sheep whom he leads" (1 Peter 2:25; Ephesians 4:11). Ellicott looks ahead to the wider fulfilment in the sending of the Seventy, whose number symbolizes "the nations of the world," the first step toward the "other sheep" of v.16.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke draws out "the marks, qualities, and duties of a good pastor": the first mark is "a lawful entrance into the ministry by the internal call of Christ," an impulse of his Spirit "upon motives which aim at nothing but the good of his Church, the salvation of souls, the doing the will of God." Gill: he "comes not of himself, but is sent," does not "thrust in himself" or "assume an office to himself," but is called and invested.

The Reformed voice (Calvin) keeps God as the judge of the door: "there are others whom the world generally applauds... but God, who holds the reins of government, does not acknowledge or approve of any but those" who enter rightly. So the witnesses agree the verse states the test of a true under-shepherd — a divine call, a lawful entrance through Christ, and care for the flock rather than for self — the very opposite of the thief who climbs the wall (v.1).

The witnesses thus make the verse the positive counterpart to the thief of v.1: where the robber climbs the wall for plunder, the true shepherd comes through the appointed door and bears the marks of a divine call. Calvin's reminder that God, not human applause, certifies the shepherd, and Clarke's catalogue of pastoral marks, together set a standard that searches every claim to spiritual authority — the test Christ presses on the Pharisees who had cast out the healed man (John 9:34) without any such warrant.
3:
"To him the porter openeth; and the sheep hear his voice: and he calleth his own sheep by name, and leadeth them out." The witnesses read the marks of the true shepherd's access and intimate care, and find rich meaning in the porter, the voice, and the naming.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the picture. Barnes: "the porter is the doorkeeper," employed by wealthier owners; the sheep "readily discern the well-known voice." Robertson: the porter "has charge of the sheep in the fold at night and opens the door in the morning," and doubts "that Jesus meant this detail to have a special application." Clarke (with Newton) sets the scene near the temple sheep-market. Lightfoot ties the porter to those who "gather the flock into the fold" (Ecclesiastes 12:11).

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices press the spiritual sense. Wesley boldly identifies the doorkeeper with the Father: "as it is not unworthy of Christ to be styled the door... so neither is it unworthy of God the Father to be styled the doorkeeper" (Acts 14:27; Colossians 4:3; Acts 16:14). Calvin reads the whole of a ministry where "God should be speaking by them."

The Latin father's note (gathered at v.1) governs "calleth his own sheep by name": Augustine, "he knew the names of the predestinated" (Luke 10:20), and "leads them out" by loosing "the chain of their sins, that they may follow him with free unfettered step." The Catena adds that the shepherd, unlike others, "goes before" the sheep "to lead all to the truth." So the witnesses converge: the true shepherd has rightful access, a voice his own sheep know, and a personal, name-by-name knowledge of each — the tender care the next verses unfold (v.4).

The witnesses thus gather three marks of the genuine relation: rightful access through the porter, a voice the sheep recognize, and a knowledge so personal it calls each by name. Augustine's reading — that the naming reaches to 'the names of the predestinated' (Luke 10:20) and the leading-out frees them from their sins — lifts the homely picture into the doctrine of grace, while Barnes and Robertson keep it anchored in the literal practice of the eastern fold; together they prepare for the shepherd who walks before his flock (v.4).
4:
"And when he putteth forth his own sheep, he goeth before them, and the sheep follow him: for they know his voice." The witnesses dwell on the shepherd going before the flock — the eastern custom that pictures Christ's leading, not driving.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the detail and the textual note. Ellicott: the better manuscripts add "all" — "when he has put forth all his own sheep" — "marking the care of the shepherd to count his flock and see that none is missing"; "put forth" is stronger than "lead out." Robertson: he "overlooks none"; the verb can mean "thrust out" a reluctant sheep that lingers. Barnes: "he went before them to seek the best pastures... and to defend them from danger," picturing "the tender care of him who watches for souls."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke makes it a mark of the faithful pastor: "he gives them a good example; he not only preaches, but he lives, the truth of the Gospel," having himself "explored the path." Wesley applies it to every minister: "he goeth before them in all the ways of God, teaching... by example as well as by precept," and the sheep follow "having the witness in themselves that his words are the wisdom and the power of God" — adding the searching question, "Reader, art thou a shepherd of souls?"

The Reformed voice (Calvin) keeps the accent on the voice that must be God's, not man's. So the witnesses agree: the true shepherd leads from the front, by example and by a voice the sheep have learned to trust, so that following him is the natural mark of belonging — the very thing a stranger's voice can never command (v.5).

The witnesses thus find in the eastern custom a picture of Christ's whole manner with his own: he does not drive from behind but leads from the front, having himself gone the way first. Wesley's searching application to every pastor — 'art thou a shepherd of souls?' — and Clarke's mark that the true minister 'lives the truth of the Gospel' bind the figure to the work of ministry, while the sheep's glad following rests, as Barnes notes, on a learned trust in the shepherd's voice (v.3).
5:
"And a stranger will they not follow, but will flee from him: for they know not the voice of strangers." The witnesses read the sheep's instinctive recoil from a strange voice as the mark of true disciples, who cannot be led away by false teachers.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the figure. Ellicott: "the stranger is any one other than their own shepherd," not to be limited to the thief of v.1; on the road they meet others "whom they would not follow." Robertson notes the strong double negative ou mē with the future — they will by no means follow "one belonging to another." Barnes: "accustomed to the voice... of a kind shepherd, they would not regard the command of a stranger" — true also spiritually, for "humble and devoted Christians" will not follow "the proud and self-seeking, as were the Pharisees" (Isaiah 1:3).

The Wesleyan-Arminian voices press the spiritual discernment. Clarke: a man who "is a stranger to that salvation which he professes to preach" is detected by his very preaching — those "whose hearts are acquainted with the truths of God" know him for a thief who got in "by an improper way." Wesley: the sheep flee a stranger "as from the plague," for "they cannot relish" his harsh, grating voice — "they find nothing of God therein."

The Reformed voice (Gill) names the stranger as "one that knows not Christ, is not sent by him, and who does not preach him." So the witnesses agree: the sheep's safety lies not in their own strength but in a trained ear — they know the true voice so well that the false repels them, the very discernment the Spirit gives to Christ's own (v.27).

The witnesses thus locate the sheep's safety not in their own strength but in a trained ear: knowing the true voice so intimately that the false repels them. Wesley's vivid 'they flee as from the plague' and Calvin's note that the stranger is one 'not sent by Christ' together teach that discernment of false teachers is itself a gift of belonging to the flock — the very mark that the Pharisees, by their voice, betrayed they did not bear (v.8).
6:
"This parable spake Jesus unto them: but they understood not what things they were which he spake unto them." The witnesses read the Pharisees' incomprehension as willful, and note the precise word the Evangelist uses for the saying.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the term. Ellicott and Robertson: the word is not the Synoptic parabolē but paroimia, "a wayside saying," a proverb or allegory — used by John here and at 16:25, 29, and in 2 Peter 2:22; "St. John nowhere uses the word parable." Barnes: "they did not understand the meaning or design of the illustration."

The Latin father turns it to a law of revelation. Augustine: "our Lord feeds by plain words, exercises by obscure" — the same words leave one hearer humbly seeking and another scornfully dismissing, so that the obscurity sifts the heart. The Reformed voice (Calvin) is blunter: "proudly vaunting of their wisdom, they rejected the light of Christ; for in a matter not very obscure they are exceedingly dull of apprehension."

The Puritan Henry draws the lesson: "many who hear the word of Christ do not understand it, because they will not" — yet "we shall find one scripture expounding another, and the blessed Spirit making known the blessed Jesus." Wesley notes "them" are the Pharisees of the close of the foregoing chapter. So the witnesses agree: the failure was not in the figure but in the hearers, whose pride dulled them — which is why Christ now drops the veil and speaks plainly, "I am the door" (v.7).

The witnesses thus turn the Pharisees' incomprehension into a lesson on revelation: the figure was not too dark for honest hearts, but pride dulled them, so that, as Augustine says, the same words feed the humble and merely exercise the proud. Henry's caution — 'they do not understand, because they will not' — and Calvin's that they were 'dull in a matter not very obscure' together explain why Christ now drops the veil and speaks the plain claim, 'I am the door' (v.7).

The contrast with the believing crowd beyond Jordan (v.42) is already implicit: the same words that baffle the proud will be received as truth by humbler hearts.
7:
"Then said Jesus unto them again, Verily, verily, I say unto you, I am the door of the sheep." The witnesses read Christ now dropping the veil of the allegory and naming himself its centre — the one lawful entrance to the fold.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the move. Ellicott: "it is not that a new allegory begins"; having spoken of door and shepherd (vv.1–2), "he now proceeds to unfold the meaning of both," saying what follows "because they did not understand." Robertson: "the door for the sheep by which they enter," the "legitimate door of access to the Fold of the House of Israel." Barnes: "the way by which ministers and people enter the true church" (Ephesians 2:18).

The Eastern father marvels at the reopening. Chrysostom: "the very door which he had shut up, he opens; he is the door." Lightfoot contrasts the old Israel, where descent from Abraham sufficed to be a sheep, with "Christ's flock," where "the sheep had another original, introduction, and mark."

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices press that all spiritual access runs through Christ. Calvin: "the principal point of all spiritual doctrine, on which souls are fed, consists in Christ" — with Paul, "I reckon nothing to be worth knowing but Jesus Christ" (1 Corinthians 2:2). Wesley: "Christ is both the Door and the Shepherd, and all things"; Cyril of Jerusalem joins the witness to eternal life through him. So the witnesses agree: the figure now becomes a claim — there is one door, and Christ is it, both for the sheep who enter and the shepherds who serve (v.9).

The witnesses thus mark the moment the allegory becomes a claim: Christ names himself the one door, so that all access to the fold — for sheep and shepherds alike — runs through him. Chrysostom's marvel that 'the door he had shut he now opens,' and Calvin's that all saving doctrine 'consists in Christ' (1 Cor 2:2), together press the exclusiveness the next verses will sharpen against every thief who climbed in another way (v.8).
8:
"All that ever came before me are thieves and robbers: but the sheep did not hear them." The witnesses agree the words cannot mean the true prophets, and debate just whom they do condemn.

All schools guard the prophets. Gill: "not of Moses, nor of the prophets of the Lord, nor of John the Baptist... but of those prophets who came, and were not sent of God." Lightfoot: Christ speaks "agreeably with the Scripture," which accuses the false shepherds who "do not feed but destroy" (Jeremiah 23:1; Ezekiel 34:2; Zechariah 11:16). The grammatical-historical readers note the textual question: Robertson and Ellicott observe that some authorities omit "before me," but the balance keeps it, the reference being to "false Messiahs and self-appointed leaders."

The witnesses divide over the precise target. The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke renders "all that came instead of me," i.e. the false Christs like Theudas and Judas the Galilean (Acts 5:36-37), "thieves, plundering the country." Barnes rather refers it to "the scribes and Pharisees, who claimed to be instructors of the people." The Reformed Calvin broadens it: "he contrasts all false doctrine, in general, with the Gospel, and all false prophets with faithful teachers," not unreasonably extending it even to the Gentile deceivers.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley aims it at the Pharisees' own pretensions — those who "pretend... to a power over the consciences of men, attempting to make laws in the Church, and to teach their own traditions as the way of salvation," running "before I have sent them by my Spirit." So the witnesses agree on the principle — every self-appointed guide who bypasses Christ the door is a robber — while ranging over false Messiahs, the Pharisees, or false teachers generally as its reach; and all note the saving clause, "the sheep did not hear them" (v.5).

The witnesses thus agree on the principle while ranging over its targets: every self-appointed guide who bypasses Christ the door is a robber, whether the false Messiahs of Clarke's reading, the Pharisees of Wesley's, or all false teachers of Calvin's. All carefully exempt the true prophets, whom the Scripture itself sets over against the destroying shepherds (Ezekiel 34:2), and all rest on the saving clause that the true sheep, with their trained ear (v.5), 'did not hear them.'
9:
"I am the door: by me if any man enter in, he shall be saved, and shall go in and out, and find pasture." The witnesses read the repeated claim now joined to a threefold promise — salvation, liberty, and provision — for all who enter by Christ.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the exclusiveness and the idiom. Robertson: "by me if any man enter in" — the emphatic di' emou — "one can call this narrow intolerance, if he will, but it is the narrowness of truth. If Jesus is the Son of God sent for our salvation, he is the only way." Ellicott parallels the "strait gate" (Matthew 7:13-14; Romans 5:2; Ephesians 2:18): none can enter or shepherd "who does not seek to do so through the character and life and death" of Christ. Barnes and Clarke note "go in and out" as a Hebrew idiom for "all the actions of a man's life" and the liberty of one well supplied and defended (John 5:24).

The Reformed voice draws out the assurance. Calvin: "the highest consolation of believers is that, when they have once embraced Christ, they learn that they are out of danger"; the promise divides into two — "they shall go safely wherever they find necessary; and they shall be fed to the full."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke and Wesley unfold the threefold gift: he "shall be saved — shall have his sins blotted out, his soul purified, and himself preserved unto eternal life," entering "through faith," kept "from the wolf," and finding "food for his soul in all circumstances." So the witnesses agree: the door is not only the way in but the guarantee of safety and pasture — Christ both admits and sustains his flock, the very abundance the next verse contrasts with the thief's ruin (v.10).

The witnesses thus hear in the repeated 'I am the door' both an exclusive claim and a generous promise: the only way in is also the guarantee of safety and pasture. Robertson's 'narrowness of truth' and Calvin's 'highest consolation' that the believer, once in the fold, is 'out of danger' together hold the two notes — the door is strait, but those who enter find life, liberty, and food, the very abundance set against the thief's threefold ruin (v.10).
10:
"The thief cometh not, but for to steal, and to kill, and to destroy: I am come that they might have life, and that they might have it more abundantly." The witnesses set the thief's threefold ruin against Christ's gift of abundant life.

The grammatical-historical readers weigh the contrast. Robertson notes the order of verbs — "stealing is the purpose of the thief, but he will kill and destroy if necessary." Barnes: "false teachers have no other end in view but to enrich or aggrandize themselves"; "more abundantly" denotes "that which is superadded to make life happy," an overflowing fulness. Ellicott: the thief's "visits are but rare, and when he comes it is but for his own selfish purposes."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke turns the thief on mercenary clergy: "those who enter into the priesthood that they may enjoy the revenues of the Church are the basest and vilest of thieves"; "their doctrine is deadly... they cannot profit the people." Wesley: "nothing else can be the consequence of a shepherd's coming, who does not enter in by me" (v.1).

The Reformed and Puritan voices read the verse as a summons to vigilance and a pledge of grace. Calvin: Christ "pulls our ear, that the ministers of Satan may not come upon us by surprise, when we are in a drowsy state," for "our excessive indifference exposes us to false doctrines." Henry: "bad principles are the root of bad practices," yet "the Lord Jesus knows whom he has chosen, and is sure of them." So the witnesses agree: where the false shepherd takes life, Christ gives it — and gives it to the full, the very life he will purchase by laying down his own (v.11).

The witnesses thus set the verse's two halves in sharpest contrast: the thief comes to take life, Christ to give it 'more abundantly.' Calvin's warning that drowsy indifference exposes us to Satan's ministers, and Clarke's against mercenary clergy whose 'doctrine is deadly,' together press vigilance, while Barnes's 'superadded' fulness and Henry's assurance that 'the Lord knows whom he has chosen' together point to the overflowing life Christ will purchase by laying down his own (v.11).
11:
"I am the good shepherd: the good shepherd giveth his life for the sheep." The witnesses read the centre of the discourse: Christ names himself the good shepherd, and at once defines the goodness by his self-sacrifice.

The grammatical-historical readers weigh the word. Robertson: "the shepherd the good one" — kalos, not merely agathos, "calls attention to the beauty in character and service"; the metaphor of v.2 now fully claimed. Ellicott: "good" means "that which is fair, and beautiful," the ideal shepherd. Barnes: "to give his life here means... to be willing to expose his life, if necessary, to defend them" — he will not fly when the wolf comes.

The Latin father binds it to the door. Augustine: "our Lord has acquainted us with two things... first, that he is the Door; and now again, that he is the Shepherd" — and the good shepherd is known because he gives his life, distinguishing him from the hireling. Cyril of Jerusalem joins the confession of the "One Lord Jesus Christ, the Only-Begotten Son of God."

The Reformed voice presses the love. Calvin: "from the extraordinary affection which he bears towards the sheep, he shows how truly he acts as a shepherd," so anxious for their salvation "that he does not even spare his own life" — so that "they who reject the guardianship of so kind a shepherd are exceedingly ungrateful." Gill: a shepherd "of his Father's appointing," who "under the character of a shepherd, died for them, and rose again." So the witnesses agree: the goodness of this shepherd is no sentiment but a death — the laying down of his life for the sheep, the theme the next verses set against the hireling's flight (v.12).

The witnesses thus define the shepherd's goodness not by sentiment but by sacrifice: the 'beautiful' shepherd (kalos) is known because he gives his life. Augustine's binding of Door and Shepherd in one person, and Calvin's that such love 'does not even spare his own life,' together make the verse the discourse's heart, and frame the gross ingratitude of rejecting so kind a guardian — the theme the hireling's flight will throw into relief (v.12).
12:
"But he that is an hireling... seeth the wolf coming, and leaveth the sheep, and fleeth." The witnesses read the hireling as the foil to the good shepherd — one who serves for wages and so abandons the flock in danger.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the figure. Robertson: "the hireling and not being a shepherd" (Mark 1:20). Ellicott: the word "implies a lower position than the household servant... what we should call the tramp-labourer," who "has no interest in the sheep." Barnes: it "denotes one who is unfaithful to his trust; especially those ministers who preach only for support, and who are unwilling" to risk themselves for the flock.

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices carefully distinguish the hireling from the worthy minister. Gill: not "the faithful ministers of the word, who live upon the Gospel, as Christ has ordained," and "are worthy of their reward." Calvin: even those who "retain the pure doctrine" yet serve "to serve a purpose rather than from pure zeal" we ought still to hear, for Christ bade men hear those who "sit in Moses' seat" (Matthew 23:2). Wesley draws the line sharply: "it is not the bare receiving hire which denominates a man a hireling... but the loving the hire more than the work," for "the labourer is worthy of his hire" (Luke 10:7).

Clarke warns against applying the censure to any "particular class or order of men: every religious party may have a hireling priest... and where the provision is the greatest, there the danger is most." So the witnesses agree: the mark of the hireling is not that he is paid but that he loves the pay above the flock, and so flees when love would cost him something — the very test the wolf applies (v.13).

The witnesses thus draw a careful line: the hireling's fault is not that he is paid but that he loves the pay above the flock and so flees when love would cost him. Wesley's precise distinction — receiving hire does not make a hireling, but 'loving the hire more than the work' — and Calvin's that the day of contest exposes such treachery, together guard the worthy minister (Luke 10:7) while condemning the self-serving heart the wolf will lay bare (v.13).
13:
"The hireling fleeth, because he is an hireling, and careth not for the sheep." A short verse, and the witnesses read it as the root cause of the hireling's flight — his heart is not in the flock.

The grammatical-historical readers note the text and the sense. Ellicott observes that the opening words "the hireling fleeth" are, on the best authorities, an addition, the verse better joined to the parenthesis of v.12; either way the point stands. Robertson: "and only that, without the shepherd heart that loves the sheep" — "it is no care to him about the sheep." Barnes: "he regards only his wages; he feels no special interest in the flock."

The Reformed voice names the test. Calvin: his heart "is not moved by the scattering of the flock, because he thinks that it does not at all belong to him"; one who "looks to the wages, and not to the flock, though he may deceive others when the Church is in a state of tranquillity, yet when he comes into the contest, will give proof of his treachery." Lightfoot adds the rabbinic background: the hired keeper was bound to watch only so far, not against "the wolf or the lion" that came by force.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley sums it: "because he loves the hire, not the sheep." So the witnesses agree: the hireling's flight is no accident of courage but the fruit of a divided heart — the day of danger merely reveals what was always true, that he served himself, not the sheep, the exact opposite of the shepherd who knows and is known (v.14).

The witnesses thus trace the flight to its root: a heart not in the flock. Calvin's verdict that the hireling, looking 'to the wages and not to the flock,' betrays himself when 'he comes into the contest,' and Wesley's plain 'he loves the hire, not the sheep,' together teach that danger does not create unfaithfulness but reveals it. The contrast is total with the shepherd who knows and is known, whose love will be proved precisely by not fleeing (v.14).
14:
"I am the good shepherd, and know my sheep, and am known of mine." The witnesses read the repeated claim now deepened into mutual knowledge — a knowing that is love and shared life between shepherd and flock.

The grammatical-historical readers press the depth of "know." Barnes: the word is used "in the sense of affectionate regard or love," so deep a knowledge of their wants and dangers "as to result in a deep interest in their welfare" — "knoweth" in this verse is explained by "loveth" in v.17. Clarke: "I know their hearts, their wishes... and I approve of them." Ellicott: not merely that the sheep know his voice, "but they partake of his nature." Robertson: "here, in this mutually reciprocal knowledge, lies the secret of their love."

The Eastern and Latin fathers raise the dignity of the office. Chrysostom: "a great matter it is to preside over a Church — a matter needing wisdom and courage as great as that of which Christ speaketh, that a man should lay down his life for the sheep." Cyril of Jerusalem joins the creed of the "Only-Begotten Son of God."

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices keep the warmth and the discrimination. Calvin: the knowing "proceeds from love, and is accompanied by care," while "he utterly disregards all who do not obey the Gospel." Wesley: "I know my sheep with a tender regard and special care, and am known of mine with a holy confidence and affection." So the witnesses agree: the bond between Christ and his own is not bare acquaintance but a mutual, loving knowledge — which the next verse will dare to liken to the knowledge between the Father and the Son (v.15).

The witnesses thus deepen the figure from care to communion: the shepherd's knowing is loving, and the sheep, as Ellicott says, 'partake of his nature.' Chrysostom's reminder of the weight of the office — that to shepherd a church is to be ready to die for it — and Calvin's that the knowing 'proceeds from love and is accompanied by care,' together prepare the daring comparison of the next verse, where this mutual knowledge is likened to that between the Father and the Son (v.15).
15:
"As the Father knoweth me, even so know I the Father: and I lay down my life for the sheep." The witnesses read the mutual knowledge of shepherd and flock now grounded in the very knowledge between Father and Son, and crowned again by the atoning death.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the connection. Ellicott: the verse-division obscures it, but the words run on from v.14 — "I know those who are mine... as the Father knoweth me" — the mutual knowledge of the sheep likened to the divine. Robertson: "and I know the Father" — "hence he is qualified to reveal the Father" (John 1:18; Matthew 11:27; John 17:21-26); "we cannot claim such perfect knowledge as exists between the Father and the Son, and yet the real sheep do know the Shepherd."

The Reformed voice warns against over-curiosity. Calvin: "it is unnecessary, and not even expedient, that we should enter into those thorny questions, how the Father knows his Wisdom"; Christ "simply declares that, so far as he is the bond of our union with God, he is placed between him and us," so that "it is no more possible for him to forget us than that he should be rejected by the Father."

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian voices return to the death. Barnes: "I lay down my life for the sheep — I give my life as an atoning sacrifice for their sins; I die in their place" (John 15:13; Isaiah 53:4-5). Lightfoot recalls how "Judah gave up his life for Benjamin." Wesley: "I lay down my life — speaking of the present time, for his whole life was only a going unto death." So the witnesses agree: the shepherd's knowledge of his sheep flows from his knowledge of the Father, and both are sealed in his dying for them — a death he will now declare wholly voluntary (v.17).

The witnesses thus ground the believer's knowledge of Christ in the very life of God: as the Father and Son know each other, so the Shepherd and his sheep. Calvin's caution against 'thorny questions' about that divine knowing, and Robertson's that 'we cannot claim such perfect knowledge,' together keep reverence, while the verse's close returns to the cross — the laying down of life that seals the bond and that the next verses declare wholly free (v.18).
16:
"And other sheep I have, which are not of this fold: them also I must bring... and there shall be one flock, and one shepherd." The witnesses read, with near unanimity, the calling of the Gentiles into one flock under Christ.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers fix the reference. Ellicott recalls the Jews' own scornful question at this very season — "Will he go unto the dispersed among the Gentiles?" (John 7:35) — and answers it: among the Gentiles "he already possesses sheep," as he later told Paul of Corinth, "I have much people in this city" (Acts 18:10). Robertson: "his flock is not confined to the Jewish fold"; "Christ's horizon takes in all men of all races and times" (John 11:52; John 12:32; Matthew 8:11). Clarke: the other sheep are "the Gentiles and Samaritans," for he is to "taste death for every man" (Hebrews 2:9).

The Reformed voice agrees while noting the figure. Calvin: "I have no doubt that he had in his eye the calling of the Gentiles," the Jewish "fold" being the ancient people separated as God's heritage, now to be joined by the nations.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley corrects a famous misprint and reads the unity precisely: "there shall be one flock (not one fold, a plain false print) — no corrupt or divided flocks remaining — and one shepherd." Barnes notes the present tense "I have": the purpose is so sure that those not yet his are spoken of as already his own. So the witnesses agree: across the Jewish fold Christ already owns Gentile sheep, and his death will gather Jew and Gentile into a single flock under a single shepherd — the great-hearted scope of the cross (v.11).

The witnesses thus hear in 'other sheep' the answer to the Jews' own scornful question about going to the Gentiles (John 7:35): Christ already owns sheep beyond the Jewish fold, and his death will gather Jew and Gentile into one flock. Wesley's careful correction — 'one flock, not one fold' — guards the unity from being read as a single visible enclosure, while Calvin and Robertson together affirm the world-wide scope of the cross (John 11:52).
17:
"Therefore doth my Father love me, because I lay down my life, that I might take it again." The witnesses read the Father's love resting on the Son's free self-offering — and the laying down already bound to the taking again.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the ground of the love. Robertson: the dia touto points forward to the clause — "the Father's love for the Son is drawn out by the voluntary offering of the Son for the sin of the world" (John 3:16; Romans 5:8; Philippians 2:9). Ellicott: "the reason of the Father's love is based upon the self-devotion of the Son" — and the laying down is "that I might take it again," the resurrection already in view.

The Reformed voice guards against misunderstanding. Calvin: "there is a higher reason why the Father loves the Son," the eternal good-pleasure (Matthew 3:17; Matthew 17:5); but "as he was made man on our account... we need not wonder if he declares the reason to be that our salvation is dearer to him" than his own life. Gill: Christ was loved "from all eternity" as his own Son, yet "also as Mediator," even in his humiliation.

The Wesleyan-Arminian voices keep the death purposeful. Clarke: "do not suppose that I shall be put to death because I have not strength to resist" — the Father loves him "because I am going to lay down my life for the life of the world." Wesley: "I cheerfully die to expiate the sins of men, to the end I may rise again for their justification." So the witnesses agree: the Son's death is no defeat but the very act in which the Father's love rests, undertaken in freedom and aimed at resurrection — the voluntariness the next verse makes explicit (v.18).

The witnesses thus find the ground of the Father's love in the Son's free self-offering, already pointing past death to resurrection ('that I might take it again'). Calvin's balance — a higher eternal love behind this, yet our salvation made 'dearer to him than his own life' — and Wesley's 'I cheerfully die... that I may rise again for their justification,' together keep the death purposeful and victorious, never a defeat, as the next verse will insist on its complete voluntariness (v.18).
18:
"No man taketh it from me, but I lay it down of myself. I have power to lay it down, and I have power to take it again." The witnesses read the strongest assertion of the wholly voluntary nature of Christ's death — and of his power over his own resurrection.

The grammatical-historical readers press the freedom. Robertson: John represents Jesus "speaking," his death "as already past and the resurrection as already accomplished"; "the voluntariness of the death of Jesus repeated and sharpened." Barnes: "no one could take it by force, unless I was willing" — he escaped the Pharisees, went up to Jerusalem knowing he would die, and told Pilate "thou couldest have no power at all against me, except it were given thee" (John 19:11). Ellicott leaves "no one taketh it" in its full width, "for it may be that even the Father is included."

The Reformed voice makes it a consolation. Calvin: "he does not die by constraint, but offers himself willingly for the salvation of his flock"; he is "free from every violence of necessity" — "it is otherwise with us, for we are laid under a necessity" of dying.

The Wesleyan-Arminian and Eastern voices keep the obedience joined to the freedom. Wesley: "by my own free act and deed... an original power and right of myself, both to lay it down as a ransom and to take it again," yet "this commission have I received of my Father." Origen and Cyril of Jerusalem dwell on the willing passion — "he came to his passion willingly," foretelling it beforehand. So the witnesses agree: the cross was no helpless overthrow but a sovereign self-giving, laid down and resumed by Christ's own power under the Father's command — the claim that will provoke the division now recorded (v.19).

The witnesses thus press the strongest note in the discourse: the cross was a sovereign self-giving, not a helpless overthrow. Robertson's reading of the death 'as already past and the resurrection as already accomplished,' and Calvin's consolation that Christ is 'free from every violence of necessity' where we are bound to die, together make the Saviour's death the freest of acts — laid down and resumed by his own power 'of myself,' yet under the Father's command, the claim that splits his hearers (v.19).
19:
"There was a division therefore again among the Jews for these sayings." The witnesses read the recurring split that the discourse provokes — the same effect Christ's words have throughout John.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the word and the pattern. Robertson: schisma again, as in 7:43 among the crowd, now "among the hostile Jews (Pharisees)"; "division of opinion about Jesus is a common thing in John's Gospel" (6:52; 9:16). Clarke: "a schism, a rent... one part received the light, and the other resisted it," referring back to the like division at John 9:16. Ellicott notes the Pharisees identifying themselves as "leaders of the party who were the enemies" of Christ.

The Puritan Henry frames the contrast: "it is hard to say which is more strange, the gracious words that came out of his mouth or the spiteful ones that came out of theirs"; the sayings "threw them into heats and parties."

The Reformed voice draws the lesson. Calvin: the discourse "procured him some disciples," but "as his doctrine has also many adversaries, hence arises division, so that they are split into parties who formerly appeared to be one body" — for all had professed to worship the God of Abraham, "but now, when Christ comes forward, they begin to differ." So the witnesses agree: the Good Shepherd's words, like the light, divide — drawing some and hardening others — the same schism that the next verses voice in the cry "he hath a devil" against "these are not the words of one possessed" (vv.20–21).

The witnesses thus read the recurring schism as the word's own effect: like the light, the Good Shepherd's sayings draw some and harden others. Calvin's observation that the doctrine 'procured him some disciples' even as it made 'many adversaries,' and Henry's contrast of his 'gracious words' with their 'spiteful ones,' together prepare the two voices that follow — the slander 'he hath a devil' against the sober witness of the better party (vv.20–21).

So the schism is not incidental but characteristic, the predictable wake of a ministry that forces every hearer to a verdict (John 9:39).
20:
"Many of them said, He hath a devil, and is mad; why hear ye him?" The witnesses read the majority's verdict — the standard slander against Christ, meant to stop men's ears.

The grammatical-historical readers note the recurrence. Robertson: "as some had already said" (John 7:20; John 8:48, with "Samaritan" added), and earlier even his friends (Mark 3:21) — "an easy way of discounting Jesus." Clarke observes that, for the Jews, "a demoniac and a madman were not exactly the same... they believed him to be possessed by a demon, who deranged" the mind. Ellicott: "and is mad" is "explanatory of the possession by a demon." Gill adds the rabbinic notion that madness came from an evil spirit.

The Reformed voice exposes the motive. Calvin: "they employ the most offensive reproach which they can devise... that all may shudder at the thought of hearing him"; wicked men, "that they may not be forced to yield to God, in a furious manner... break out into proud contempt of him, and excite others to the same rage, so that not a single word of Christ is heard in silence." Yet, he adds, "the doctrine of Christ has sufficient power" to stand.

So the witnesses agree: the charge of madness and possession was no argument but a tactic — to silence inquiry by branding the speaker, the very thing that drove some to dismiss him unheard while others paused to weigh his words (v.21).

The witnesses thus expose the charge of madness as a tactic, not an argument: by branding the speaker possessed, they hoped to stop men's ears. Robertson's catalogue of its repetition (7:20; 8:48) and Calvin's that wicked men 'excite others to the same rage, so that not a single word of Christ is heard in silence,' together show why the slander recurs — and why, against it, the better party will appeal to his calm words and his undeniable miracle (v.21).

It is the easiest of evasions and the oldest, and it spares the accuser the labour of weighing what was actually said (John 8:48).
21:
"Others said, These are not the words of him that hath a devil. Can a devil open the eyes of the blind?" The witnesses read the better party's reply — sober words and the recent miracle answering the charge of madness.

The grammatical-historical readers weigh the two arguments. Robertson: they had "heard demoniacs talk, but not like this"; and "can a demon open the eyes of the blind?" — "demons would more likely put out eyes, not open them... an unanswerable question." Ellicott traces "the presence of the better party among the Sanhedrin, which we found before" (John 9:16): "his words are words of calm teaching" — "the possession by a demon disorders, frenzies" — and points back to the healing of John 9:6. Barnes: "his words are sober, grave, pious, full of wisdom."

The Reformed voice reads the appeal to fact. Calvin: "it is as if they demanded that men should judge from the fact itself; for the truth is strong enough to maintain itself" — "this is the only protection of our faith, that wicked men will never be able to hinder the power and wisdom of God... from shining in the Gospel." Wesley notes the word for "words" "takes in actions too."

The Eastern Cyril of Jerusalem joins the sign of opened eyes to the Messianic promise (Isaiah 35:5). So the witnesses agree: against the slander of v.20 stand both the character of Christ's teaching and the undeniable miracle — evidence the malicious could not answer, and which left the division unresolved until the Feast of Dedication (v.22).

The witnesses thus set two solid evidences against the slander of v.20: the sober character of Christ's teaching and the opened eyes of the man born blind (John 9:7). Calvin's appeal 'to the fact itself,' since 'the truth is strong enough to maintain itself,' and Robertson's 'unanswerable question' — can a demon open blind eyes? — together leave the malicious without reply, the division hanging unresolved until the winter feast renews the confrontation (v.22).

Thus even amid the hostile majority, the works of Christ kept some minds open, the deed of mercy refusing to be explained away (John 9:32).
22:
"And it was at Jerusalem the feast of the dedication, and it was winter." The witnesses fix the setting — the Maccabean feast of the temple's rededication — and note both its history and the fact that Christ honoured it.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed readers supply the history. Barnes and Clarke: the feast was instituted by Judas Maccabaeus in 164 B.C., after Antiochus Epiphanes had defiled the temple — slaying and enslaving the people and sacrificing a sow on the altar; it began on the 25th of Chislev (about our December) and lasted eight days. Calvin: the Greek enkainia means "renewal," the temple "again consecrated by the command of Judas Maccabaeus," the day kept yearly "that the people might recall the grace of God." Ellicott calculates the interval from Tabernacles (John 7:2) at about two months. Robertson prefers the reading "at that time came the feast."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley draws a practical inference: this feast was "of human appointment" (1 Macc. 4:59), "and yet our Lord observed festivals even of human appointment. Is it not, at least, innocent for us to do the same?"

The Latin and Eastern fathers turn to the discourse that follows. John Chrysostom: "every virtue is a good thing, but most of all gentleness and meekness" — the temper Christ shows amid their rage. Augustine and Alcuin note the "porch of Solomon" where he walked, and that he honoured a temple "where the flesh of brute animals was offered up." So the witnesses agree: the scene is the winter feast of rededication, and Christ's presence there — observing a festival of human origin — sets the stage for the sharpest confrontation yet over his identity (v.24).

The witnesses thus fix the scene at the winter feast of rededication and note its lesson: Christ, present at a festival of human institution, honoured it without scruple. Calvin's account of the temple 'again consecrated' under Judas Maccabaeus and Wesley's inference that such observance may be 'innocent for us' together set the historical frame, while the fathers turn at once to the gentleness Christ will show amid the rage that this very feast becomes the stage for (v.24).
23:
"And Jesus walked in the temple in Solomon's porch." A brief scene-setting verse, and the witnesses fix the place and its history with the care of eyewitness memory.

The grammatical-historical readers picture it. Robertson: the imperfect "was walking," a "picturesque imperfect," in "a covered colonnade or portico in which people could walk in all weather" (Acts 3:11; Acts 5:12) — the part of the temple "left uninjured by the Babylonians and survived apparently till the destruction by Titus." Ellicott: "the scene is remembered and pictured as it took place" — "rather a cloister or arcade than what we usually call a porch," on the east side, "a relic of the original building." Clarke and Wesley cite Josephus on the great eastern portico Solomon raised over the filled valley, "supported by a wall four hundred cubits high."

The Reformed voice (Calvin, Gill) notes the practical reason — Christ walked "to keep himself warm" in the winter feast (v.22) — and that the "temple" here is the later rebuilt structure bearing the old name "that it might be more highly honoured."

So the witnesses agree on a small but vivid point: the confrontation that follows happened in a real, identifiable place, the ancient eastern colonnade, on a cold winter day — the historical solidity that John's eyewitness memory preserves, and the setting where the Jews now press him for a plain word (v.24).

The witnesses thus anchor the confrontation in a real, datable place: the ancient eastern colonnade that survived from the first temple until Titus. Robertson's 'picturesque imperfect' — Jesus walking — and Ellicott's note that 'the scene is remembered and pictured as it took place' together mark the eyewitness solidity of John's record, the cold winter day (v.22) and the sheltered porch where the Jews now close in to press their demand (v.24).

So the Evangelist's brief notice of place and season is no idle detail but the frame of a decisive encounter, the sheltered porch where the question of his identity is forced to a head (v.24).
24:
"Then came the Jews round about him, and said, How long dost thou make us to doubt? If thou be the Christ, tell us plainly." The witnesses read the encircling demand as a snare more than an honest question.

The grammatical-historical readers picture the scene and weigh the phrase. Robertson: they "came round about him," ekyklōsan, encircled him, "cherishing the memory of the stinging rebuke" of the Good Shepherd allegory. Ellicott: "the impression of one who saw what he records" — they "stood in a circle round our Lord... watched him with eager eyes"; the words run literally, "How long dost thou lift up our souls?" — keeping us in suspense. Lightfoot doubts they hoped he was Messiah; "they rather feared than hoped it," looking for a political deliverer.

The Reformed voice exposes the trap. Calvin: "this was undoubtedly a cunning attack"; while the common people might "without any fraud" desire an open declaration, "a few persons, by trick and stratagem, wished to draw this word from him amidst the crowd, that he might be killed by a mob, or that the Romans might lay hold of him." Barnes notes they knew the Messiah was predicted as a shepherd, and Jesus had applied that to himself.

Clarke renders the demand sharply — "How long dost thou kill us with suspense?" — and reads their wish "to get him to declare himself king of the Jews, that they might" accuse him. (A note attributed to Jamieson, Fausset and Brown in the merged sources here addresses Herod and does not bear on this verse.) So the witnesses agree: the question wore the dress of sincerity but carried a hook — they sought not light but a pretext, which Christ's answer at once denies them (v.25).

The witnesses thus read the encircling question as a hook dressed as sincerity: 'tell us plainly' sought not light but a pretext. Calvin's exposure of the 'cunning attack' to draw a word that 'a mob, or the Romans' might use against him, and Lightfoot's note that they 'feared rather than hoped' he was Messiah, together explain why Christ answers indirectly — to say 'Messiah' in their political sense would have been to feed the very plot (v.25).
25:
"I told you, and ye believed not: the works that I do in my Father's name, they bear witness of me." The witnesses read Christ's indirect answer — he points not to a bare title but to his words and works already given.

The grammatical-historical readers explain the indirectness. Ellicott: "here, as in John 8:25... the answer is indirect. It could not be otherwise," for "their misconception of the Messianic work had made the very word Messiah an impossible one for him to utter" — to say it "would have been to sanction their thought" of a political deliverer. Robertson: in 7:14–10:18 "Jesus had shown that he was the Son of the Father," but "it was all to no purpose save to increase their rage"; "his works confirm his words" (John 5:36). Barnes lists where he had used expressions "in a sense not to be mistaken" (John 5:19; John 8:56).

The Reformed voice reads the reproach. Calvin: Christ "does not conceal that he is the Christ, and yet he does not teach them as if they were willing to learn, but rather reproaches them with obstinate malice"; that they do not know him "he imputes to their own fault" — "my doctrine is clear enough, but you are blind."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke gathers all Christ had told them — "I am the light of the world, the Son of God, the good shepherd... I am come to save, to give life, to give liberty." So the witnesses agree: Christ had answered the question many times over, in word and in deed; the failure lay not in the evidence but in their unbelief, which the next verse traces to its root (v.26).

The witnesses thus find Christ's answer already given many times over, in word and deed: 'the works that I do bear witness of me' (John 5:36). Ellicott's point that the word 'Messiah' was 'impossible' to utter given their misconception, and Calvin's that he 'imputes to their own fault' their not knowing him, together locate the failure in their unbelief, not in any want of evidence — the unbelief whose root the next verse will name (v.26).
26:
"But ye believe not, because ye are not of my sheep, as I said unto you." The witnesses read the root of unbelief — and here the chapter's election debate is at its sharpest.

The Reformed voice presses the doctrine. Calvin: "he assigns a higher reason why they do not believe... it is because they are reprobate." Christ's design is that their unbelief "may detract nothing from the authority of the Gospel": "the gift of faith is a special gift" (Galatians 4:9). Robertson, in this vein: "in fact, they were the children of the devil in spirit and conduct" (John 8:43), "veritable wolves in sheep's clothing" (Matthew 7:15).

The Wesleyan-Arminian voices read it the other way. Clarke insists "our Lord does not at all insinuate that these persons could not believe, because God had made it impossible to them; but simply because they did not hear and follow Christ" — the whole discourse proves it. Wesley: "ye do not believe, because ye are not of my sheep — because ye do not, will not follow me: because ye are proud, unholy, lovers of praise... not of God."

The grammatical-historical Barnes stands between, naming the moral cause: "were it not for pride, and prejudice, and vainglory... you would have learned" (John 8:47). So the witnesses divide on the deepest question of the chapter: is unbelief because they are not elect sheep (Calvin, Robertson), or are they "not sheep" because they refuse to follow (Wesley, Clarke)? — the same Calvinist/Arminian seam that runs through John, presented here in its starkest form, and continued in the promises of v.27.

The witnesses thus reach the chapter's sharpest seam: is unbelief because they are not of the elect sheep, or are they 'not sheep' because they refuse to follow? Calvin's 'they are reprobate' and Wesley's 'because ye will not follow me' stand on either side, with Barnes between, naming pride and prejudice as the moral cause (John 8:47). The site presents both fairly; what all share is that the marks of true sheep — hearing, following — are absent here, and present in the promise that follows (v.27).
27:
"My sheep hear my voice, and I know them, and they follow me." The witnesses read the threefold mark of Christ's true sheep, drawn from the allegory's opening, and set against the unbelief just named.

The grammatical-historical readers tie it back. Robertson: "in contrast with you they are not in doubt and suspense. They know my voice and follow me" — repeating v.4, 14. Barnes: "hear and obey his commandments," the resemblance between sheep and disciples drawn out.

The Reformed voice makes it an argument from effect. Calvin: "he proves by an argument drawn from contraries, that they are not sheep, because they do not obey the Gospel. For God effectually calls all whom he has elected, so that the sheep of Christ are proved by their faith" — the name "sheep" belongs to those who "surrender themselves to God, to be governed by the hand of the Chief Shepherd."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads the marks as conditions with promises annexed: the sheep "hear my voice by faith; are known (that is, approved) by me, as loving me; and follow me, keep my commandments, with a believing, loving heart" — "three promises annexed to three conditions" (v.28). Clarke: "My sheep hear my voice — but ye will not hear." So the witnesses agree on the marks — hearing, being known, following — while reading them, in their two manners, either as the proof of election (Calvin) or as the conditions a loving heart fulfils (Wesley); and both lead straight into the great promise of security (v.28).

The witnesses thus read the threefold mark — hearing, being known, following — either as the proof of election (Calvin's 'argument from contraries') or as the loving conditions a true heart fulfils (Wesley's 'three conditions with three promises annexed'). Either way they distinguish the real sheep from the doubting Jews of v.24, and lead straight into the security that crowns them: eternal life given, and a hand from which none can pluck them (v.28).

Either way, the verse turns from the unbelief of v.26 to the warm portrait of those who truly belong, whose hearing and following are the visible signs of an inward bond.
28:
"And I give unto them eternal life; and they shall never perish, neither shall any man pluck them out of my hand." The witnesses read the great promise of the believer's security, resting on the shepherd's own power.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the emphatic guarantee. Robertson: "this is the gift of Jesus now to his sheep" (John 6:40; 1 John 5:11); "and they shall never perish" — "emphatic double negative"; "the sheep may feel secure" (John 3:16; John 17:12; John 18:9), and "no one shall snatch them" from his hand. Barnes traces "perish" through Scripture as to be destroyed or punished in hell (Matthew 10:28; John 3:15).

The Reformed voice makes it the believer's assurance. Calvin: "it is an inestimable fruit of faith, that Christ bids us be convinced of our security when we are brought by faith into his fold"; the certainty rests on this — "he will be a faithful guardian of our salvation," guarded "by the power of God."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke joins the security to the marks of v.27: "why? because they hear my voice and follow me... they who continue to hear Christ's voice and to follow him shall never perish" — the perseverance bound to continuance. So the witnesses agree on the substance of the promise — eternal life given, never to perish, none able to pluck the sheep from Christ's hand — while Calvin reads unconditional security and Clarke ties it to continuing to hear and follow; the promise then climbs higher to the Father's hand (v.29).

The witnesses thus rest the believer's security on the Shepherd's own power: eternal life given now, an emphatic 'never perish,' and no hand able to snatch the sheep away. Calvin reads unconditional assurance — Christ 'a faithful guardian of our salvation' — while Clarke ties the keeping to continuing 'to hear and follow' (v.27). Both rest on the same promise, which the next verse lifts higher still into the omnipotent hand of the Father (v.29).

The promise is therefore as strong as the Shepherd himself, and its comfort lies precisely in that the keeping is his work and not the sheep's (John 6:39).
29:
"My Father, which gave them me, is greater than all; and no man is able to pluck them out of my Father's hand." The witnesses read the promise of security now anchored in the Father's supreme power.

The grammatical-historical readers note a real textual crux. Robertson: the manuscripts vary between "who" (the Father is greater than all) and a neuter reading yielding, "that which he hath given me is greater than all" — Westcott and Hort prefer the neuter, though Robertson questions it. Ellicott keeps "all" unlimited — "every creature and every power, and even... the Son in his subordination to the Father" — so that the Father "is thought of as greater." Barnes: "greater than all — than all others, men, angels, devils... God is supreme," and "will so control all other beings" that the sheep are safe.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke draws the comfort: the Father is "more powerful than all the united energies of men and demons. He who loves God must be happy, and he who fears him need fear nothing on this side eternity." Gill recalls how "the Father gave them to him, put them into his hands, and made them his care and charge" (John 6:37).

So the witnesses agree: whatever the precise reading, the point stands — the sheep are doubly safe, held in the Son's hand and the Father's, and the Father's omnipotence is the final guarantee that none can tear them away. This double security leads directly to the claim that binds the two hands into one (v.30).

The witnesses thus double the believer's safety: held in the Son's hand (v.28) and the Father's, who is 'greater than all.' The textual variation between 'who is greater' and 'that which he gave is greater' does not unsettle the point, which Barnes states plainly — God is supreme over 'men, angels, devils' and will keep his own. This double security then drives to its ground in the claim that binds the two hands into one (v.30).

So the believer's confidence rests not on the strength of his own grip but on the omnipotent hands that hold him, Son and Father together (Romans 8:38-39).
30:
"I and my Father are one." The witnesses read the climax of the security promise as a claim of essential unity with the Father — and one of the New Testament's great proof-texts for the deity of Christ.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers fix the grammar precisely. Robertson: "one" is neuter (hen), not masculine (heis) — "not one person... but one essence or nature"; "by the plural esmen (we are) Sabellius is refuted, by hen Arius." Wesley says the same: "are confutes Sabellius, proving the plurality of persons; one confutes Arius, proving the unity of nature" — "not by consent of will only, but by unity of power, and consequently of nature." Clarke: had Jesus not been God, could he have said this "without being guilty of blasphemy?"

The Reformed voice reads the immediate context while granting the doctrine. Calvin: "the ancients made a wrong use of this passage" to prove simply that Christ is homoousios; in context Christ "was not discussing the unity of substance, but the agreement which he has with the Father," testifying that "the Father's assistance will never be withheld from himself and his sheep" — yet the unity of operation rests on unity of nature. Ellicott: "in the infinity of All-mighty Power the Son is one with the Father."

So the witnesses agree on the doctrine — true unity of the Son with the Father, neuter "one" guarding both the distinction of persons and the unity of nature — while differing in emphasis over whether the verse argues first from essence (the fathers, Wesley) or from the shared power that secures the sheep (Calvin). The Jews, at any rate, heard it as a claim to deity, and reached for stones (v.31).

The witnesses thus read the climax as a true claim of unity with the Father, the neuter 'one' guarding both the distinction of persons and the unity of nature. Wesley's epigram — 'are confutes Sabellius, one confutes Arius' — and Calvin's reminder that the immediate point is the shared power securing the sheep, together hold doctrine and context. The Jews, hearing it as a claim to deity, reach for stones, themselves becoming witnesses to its meaning (v.31).
31:
"Then the Jews took up stones again to stone him." The witnesses read the violent response as proof that they understood his claim — and as the recurrence of an old rage.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the law and the recurrence. Barnes: "stoning was the punishment of a blasphemer" (Leviticus 24:14-16); they judged him guilty "because he made himself equal with God" (v.33); "again" recalls John 8:59. Robertson notes a different verb here — "they fetched stones from a distance" (or "carried"), where 8:59 had "lifted up." Lightfoot details the Sanhedrin's process for the blasphemer, here attempted "without judgment."

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the rage and the claim. Augustine: "at this speech, I and my Father are one, the Jews could not restrain their rage"; Hilary turns it on the heretics who, "unbelieving," deny what the Jews at least understood. Chrysostom reads Christ's following words as "a speech of humility, made to conciliate."

The Reformed voice names the spirit. Calvin: "as true religion... burns with its own zeal which the Spirit of God directs, so unbelief is the mother of rage," and "the devil hurries on the wicked" to breathe "nothing but slaughter" — "this result shows with what intention they put the question" of v.24. Henry: his works "proclaim him to be over all, God blessed for evermore." So the witnesses agree: the stones prove the Jews grasped his meaning exactly — he had claimed deity — and their fury, not any obscurity, drove them; which Christ now meets with argument (v.32).

The witnesses thus read the stones as proof that the Jews understood Christ exactly: he had claimed deity, and they judged it blasphemy (Lev 24:16). Augustine's note that 'I and my Father are one' kindled their rage, and Calvin's that 'unbelief is the mother of rage,' together show that no obscurity but only fury drove them — the same intent that lay hidden in their question (v.24), now meeting the argument Christ draws from their own Scripture (v.34).

Their reach for stones is thus a backhanded confession: they grasped the claim of v.30 far better than the heretics who would later explain it away.
32:
"Many good works have I shewed you from my Father; for which of those works do ye stone me?" The witnesses read Christ meeting the stones with an appeal to his works of mercy — and a charge of ingratitude.

The grammatical-historical readers weigh the words. Robertson: the works proceed "out of the Father" (John 16:28); "for what kind of work" — the "many good works" (kala), "noble and beautiful deeds" like the healings. Ellicott: "good" here means "excellent, distinguished" — "it is not the nature of the works as beneficent" alone but their splendour as from the Father. Barnes: "he had performed no other works than those of benevolence... and he confidently appealed to them."

The Reformed voice presses the ingratitude. Calvin: Christ "not only says that they have no reason for their cruelty, but accuses them of ingratitude, in making so unjust a requital for God's favours"; he had been kind "in many ways," and is "the minister of God" in these works — so their rage is against God. Gill lists the works: healing the sick, casting out devils, cleansing lepers, opening blind eyes — "not only works of power, but of mercy."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke catalogues the same — "I have healed your sick, delivered the possessed, fed your poor, taught you... without expense, with patience; and is this my reward?" So the witnesses agree: Christ answers violence with evidence, setting his record of beneficent, God-given works against their stones, and exposing that their quarrel is not with any evil deed but with the deity those works attest (v.33).

The witnesses thus see Christ answer violence with evidence and a charge of ingratitude: 'for which of those good works do ye stone me?' Calvin's reading — that to requite God's manifold favours with cruelty is rank ingratitude, since the works are the Father's — and Clarke's catalogue of mercies freely given, together expose that their quarrel is not with any evil deed but with the deity those beneficent works attest, the very point their own indictment will confirm (v.33).
33:
"For a good work we stone thee not; but for blasphemy; and because that thou, being a man, makest thyself God." The witnesses read the Jews' own words as the decisive proof of how they understood Christ's claim.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the charge. Robertson: "flat denial that the healing... had led them to this" — "but for blasphemy," the technical indictment (Leviticus 24:10-16). Ellicott: the word for "for" is now "concerning," "the technical form for an indictment." Barnes: "this shows how they understood what he had said. Makest thyself God — dost claim to be God, or thy language implies this" (John 5:18).

The witnesses across the schools agree that this verse is, in effect, a hostile witness to orthodox Christology: the Jews did not mistake a lesser claim for a greater, but heard rightly that "I and my Father are one" (v.30) meant deity. Clarke: "thou, being a man — that is, only a man — makest thyself God."

The Reformed voice notes the pretext. Calvin: "wicked men... never wish to sin without some plausible pretense," so they "constitute themselves advocates and defenders of the glory of God" while raging against the Son of God — "a good conscience must therefore be to us a wall of brass." So the witnesses agree: the charge itself confirms the claim — Christ had made himself God in their hearing — and the question that remains is only whether the claim is blasphemy or truth, which Christ's reply will press home from their own Scripture (v.34).

The witnesses thus take the Jews' own words as a hostile witness to orthodox Christology: they did not mistake a lesser claim for a greater, but rightly heard 'I and my Father are one' (v.30) as making himself God (John 5:18). Calvin's note that they cloak rage in zeal for God's glory shows the pretext; the substance is plain — the charge concedes the claim, leaving only the question whether it is blasphemy or truth, which Christ now presses (v.34).

So the verse remains one of the clearest contemporary witnesses that Jesus' hearers understood him to claim true and proper deity (John 5:18).
34:
"Is it not written in your law, I said, Ye are gods?" The witnesses read Christ's reply from Psalm 82:6 — an argument from the lesser to the greater, meeting them on their own Scripture.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the citation and the method. Barnes: "law" here is used "in a wide sense" for the whole Old Testament (John 12:34; John 15:25; Romans 3:19); the first part of his answer (vv.34–36) shows they should not object to his use of "God," the second (37–38) appeals again to his works. Ellicott names it "Hillel's famous First Canon of Interpretation — that the greater may be inferred from the less." Clarke and Wesley: "ye are gods" means "judges, who are called elohim" (Psalm 82:2), the magistrates who bore God's word and authority.

The Reformed voice reads the strategy. Calvin: Christ "clears himself of the crime charged against him, not by denying that he is the Son of God, but by maintaining that he had justly said so"; he "adapts his reply to the persons, instead of giving a full explanation," reckoning it "enough for the present to expose their malice." Robertson: "as Jews (and rabbis) they are shut out from charging Jesus with blasphemy because of this usage in the O.T. It is a complete argument."

So the witnesses agree: Christ does not retract the claim of v.30 but defends it — if Scripture itself called mere men "gods" because God's word came to them, the charge of blasphemy collapses against one far greater; the argument the next verses complete (v.35).

The witnesses thus see Christ meet the charge on their own ground, arguing from Scripture's own use of 'gods' for the judges to whom God's word came. Ellicott names it 'Hillel's first canon' — the greater inferred from the less — and Calvin notes he 'clears himself not by denying that he is the Son of God, but by maintaining that he had justly said so.' The argument does not retract v.30 but defends it, and the next verses complete it (v.36).
35:
"If he called them gods, unto whom the word of God came, and the scripture cannot be broken..." The witnesses read the first half of Christ's a fortiori argument, with its weighty aside on the authority of Scripture.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the premise. Barnes: those called "gods" were such because "the law of God was intrusted to them" — God's "commission to them to do justice"; and "the scripture cannot be broken" means "the authority of the Scripture is final; it cannot be set aside" (Matthew 5:19). Ellicott: "the word of God came" — "the word declaring 'Ye are gods.'" Clarke (with Bishop Pearce) reads "the word" as "the matter of judgment" entrusted to the judges (2 Chronicles 19:6).

The Reformed voice draws the doctrine of magistracy. Calvin: "empires did not spring up at random... but were appointed by the will of God," who "wishes that political order should exist among men"; hence Paul, that "all who resist the power are rebels against God" (Romans 13:1-2). Lightfoot records the rabbis' own (mistaken) reading of the Psalm.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley fixes the sense: "if he (God) called them gods unto whom the word of God came... and the Scripture cannot be broken — that is, nothing which is written therein can be censured or rejected." So the witnesses agree: the argument turns on a truth they could not deny — that Scripture is unbreakable — so that if its own inspired word styled mere judges "gods," the title cannot be blasphemy in one whom the Father consecrated and sent (v.36).

The witnesses thus rest the argument on a premise the Jews could not deny — that 'the scripture cannot be broken,' its authority final (Matthew 5:18). If the inspired word itself styled mere magistrates 'gods' for bearing God's commission, the title cannot be blasphemy in one far greater. Calvin's note that magistracy is God-appointed (Rom 13:1) and Barnes's on the unbreakable Scripture together fix the foundation on which the verse's conclusion stands (v.36).

The premise carried all the more weight because it was the Jews' own unquestioned conviction, used now to dismantle their charge from within (v.34).
36:
"Say ye of him, whom the Father hath sanctified, and sent into the world, Thou blasphemest; because I said, I am the Son of God?" The witnesses read the conclusion of the argument — Christ contrasting himself, the consecrated and sent One, with the mere judges of the Psalm.

The grammatical-historical readers fix "sanctified." Barnes: it does not mean "to make holy," for the Son was always holy, but "to set apart from a common to a sacred use; to devote... and consecrate to a holy office" — God "appointed his Son to be his Messiah." Robertson: "every word counts heavily here in contrast with the mere judges of Ps 82:6." Ellicott: the tense refers "to the time of his consecration to his Messianic work, and to the Incarnation."

The Reformed and Wesleyan-Arminian voices press the contrast upward. Calvin: "here Christ claims for himself something far more excellent" than the common sanctification of believers — "that he alone was separated from all others, that the power of the Spirit and the majesty of God might be displayed in him" (John 6:27), as manifested in the flesh. Wesley: this sanctification, "prior to his mission, implies Christ was God in the highest sense, infinitely superior to that wherein those judges were so called."

So the witnesses agree: the argument is complete — if Scripture called mere men "gods" for bearing God's word, far less can it be blasphemy for the One whom the Father set apart and sent to call himself "the Son of God." Christ does not lower his claim but vindicates it, and now stakes it on the evidence of his works (v.37).

The witnesses thus complete the a fortiori: if Scripture called mere men 'gods,' far less is it blasphemy for the One whom the Father 'sanctified and sent.' Barnes's careful sense of 'sanctified' — set apart and consecrated to office — and Wesley's that this consecration, prior to his mission, implies Christ is 'God in the highest sense,' together show that Christ does not lower his claim but vindicates it, and now stakes it on the public test of his works (v.37).
37:
"If I do not the works of my Father, believe me not." The witnesses read Christ now resting his whole claim on the test of his works — a concession that invites verification.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the logic. Robertson: a first-class condition assumed as true, with the prohibition "cease believing me" — "Jesus rests his case on his doing the works of my Father," repeating his claims "to sonship and deity." Barnes: "the works of his Father are those which God only can do. As Jesus did them, it shows that the name 'Son of God'... was properly applied to him" (John 5:17). Ellicott: "whether he is a blasphemer or not depends upon whether he represents God or not, and to prove this he appeals again to the works" (John 5:36).

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke renders the appeal plainly: "I desire you to believe only on the evidence of my works: if I do not do such works as God only can perform, then believe me not."

The Reformed voice reads it as a gracious concession. Calvin: "lest the Jews might reply that it was in vain for him to boast... he again draws their attention to his miracles, in which there was a sufficiently evident proof of his Divinity" — "I do not wish you to be bound to give me credit on any other condition than that you see the fact plainly before your eyes." So the witnesses agree: Christ stakes his claim of deity on a public, testable ground — his Father's works — so that unbelief becomes inexcusable; the positive side follows at once (v.38).

The witnesses thus see Christ rest his whole claim on a testable ground: 'if I do not the works of my Father, believe me not.' Barnes's point that 'the works of his Father are those which God only can do,' and Calvin's reading of the gracious concession — believe only on the plain evidence before your eyes — together make unbelief inexcusable, the negative condition preparing the positive appeal that the works themselves reveal the Father in the Son (v.38).
38:
"But if I do, though ye believe not me, believe the works: that ye may know and believe that the Father is in me, and I in him." The witnesses read the positive appeal — and the deep statement of mutual indwelling that interprets v.30.

The grammatical-historical readers weigh the conditions. Robertson: a first-class condition ("if I do," assumed true) joined to a third-class ("even if you keep on not believing me") — "believe the works. These stand irrefutable." Barnes: "consider my works, for they prove that I came from God"; "the Father is in me" denotes "most intimate union — such as can exist in no other case" (John 5:36; John 17:21). Clarke: the works prove "that I am in the Father and the Father in me; and consequently that I and the Father are one" — "every man must see and feel that it is conclusive."

The Reformed voice reads the convicting force. Calvin: they are "plainly convicted of unbelieving and sacrilegious contempt, because they render no reverence to what are undoubtedly the works of God" — "though I allow you to doubt of my doctrine, you cannot deny... that the miracles are from God."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley notes the order of faith and knowledge: "in some a more exact knowledge precedes, in others it follows faith"; and the two sentences — "the Father in me" and "I and the Father are one" — "illustrate each other." Cyril of Jerusalem adds Isaiah's witness that "God is in thee" (Isaiah 45:14). So the witnesses agree: the works are the bridge from unbelief to faith, and their lesson is the mutual indwelling of Father and Son — the same deity the Jews had called blasphemy (v.33).

The witnesses thus make the works the bridge from unbelief to faith, and their lesson the mutual indwelling — 'the Father is in me, and I in him' (John 17:21) — which interprets the disputed 'one' of v.30.
