Calvin's charge that they are 'convicted of sacrilegious contempt' for denying the works are God's, and Wesley's note that knowledge may precede or follow faith, together press the very deity the Jews had called blasphemy (v.33).
39:
"Therefore they sought again to take him: but he escaped out of their hand." The witnesses read the failed seizure as one more instance of Christ guarded until his hour — and the Jews hardened past argument.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the pattern. Robertson: the imperfect, "they kept on seeking to seize him," as they had tried repeatedly (7:30, 44; 8:20) — "overawed, but still angry, the stones fell to the ground, and Jesus walked" away. Ellicott: "again" refers to those earlier attempts; "nothing is said of the manner" of his escape. Barnes: "they evidently understood him as still claiming equality with God," and he left them with that impression — "if so, then he is divine."

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the violence and the withdrawal. Bede: "the Jews still persist in their madness"; Augustine, they sought "to lay hold of him, not by faith and the understanding, but with bloodthirsty violence." Theophylact: "our Lord often brings out the people into solitary places, ridding them of the society of the unbelieving, for their furtherance in the faith."

The Reformed and Puritan voices read the providence. Calvin: this "could not be accomplished in any other way than by a wonderful exertion of Divine power" — "we are not exposed to the lawless passions of wicked men, which God restrains." Henry: "one would have thought it would have convinced and melted them, but their hearts were hardened." So the witnesses agree: argument and evidence having failed to move them, they turned again to force, and again were thwarted — Christ withdrawing by God's power to a place where faith would be found (v.40).

The witnesses thus read the failed seizure as one more proof of Christ guarded until his hour, and of hearts hardened past argument. Robertson's 'they kept on seeking' as before (7:30), Calvin's that the escape was 'a wonderful exertion of Divine power,' and Henry's that the discourse should have 'melted them' but did not, together show evidence and argument failing where the will refuses — driving Christ to withdraw where faith would yet be found (v.40).
40:
"And went away again beyond Jordan into the place where John at first baptized; and there he abode." The witnesses read Christ's withdrawal to the scene of the Baptist's early ministry — a retreat for ministry, not idleness.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the place. Robertson: "again" refers to John 1:28 (Bethany beyond Jordan), using "the identical language" with the added "at the first"; "and there he abode" (imperfect). Ellicott connects it with "the coasts of Judaea beyond Jordan" (Matthew 19:1). Clarke notes John later baptized at Aenon (John 3:23), this being the earlier site, Bethabara. Lightfoot: "that is, Bethabara."

The Reformed voice reads the purpose. Calvin: "Christ passed beyond Jordan that he might not have to fight continually without any advantage"; by his example he teaches "that we ought to avail ourselves of opportunities, when they occur" — withdrawing not to rest but "to erect a sanctuary of God in the wilderness, when Jerusalem... had obstinately driven him out." Gill: he went "not merely for the security of his person, much less to indulge himself in ease, but to preach the Gospel, work miracles, and bring many souls to believe."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley notes the fitness — the desert place "where John baptized, and gave so honourable a testimony of him." So the witnesses agree: Christ's retreat is strategic and fruitful, returning to the ground hallowed by the Baptist's witness, where, far from the murderous city, a harvest of faith awaits (v.41).

The witnesses thus read the withdrawal beyond Jordan as strategic and fruitful, not a retreat into ease. Calvin's striking image — Christ 'erecting a sanctuary of God in the wilderness' when Jerusalem had driven him out — and Gill's that he went 'to preach the Gospel, work miracles, and bring many souls to believe,' together turn the geography into theology: the rejected Shepherd gathers his flock on the ground hallowed by the Baptist's witness (John 1:28), where a harvest waits (v.42).

So the place of the Baptist's first witness becomes the place of a late harvest, the ministry coming full circle to where it had been heralded (John 1:29).
41:
"And many resorted unto him, and said, John did no miracle: but all things that John spake of this man were true." The witnesses read the vindication of the Baptist's witness — confirmed now that the people see the One he foretold.

The grammatical-historical readers mark the contrast. Robertson: "Jesus was busy here and in a more congenial atmosphere than Jerusalem"; though "John wrought no signs the crowds recall, Jesus did many here" (Matthew 19:2) — "the crowds still bear the impress of John's witness to Christ as true"; "here was prepared soil for Christ." Barnes: "either miracle or prophecy is conclusive proof of a divine mission" — John "showed that he was a prophet by foretelling the character and success of Jesus." Ellicott hears "a key-note of this Gospel" (John 1:5): "in the midst of even the deepest darkness the light is never absent."

The Reformed voice reads the providence. Calvin: "this large assembly shows that Christ did not seek solitude in order to cease from his duty, but to erect a sanctuary of God in the wilderness," while Jerusalem, "his own abode," had driven him out — "a dreadful vengeance of God."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley notes the fitting limit: "John did no miracle — an honour reserved for him whose forerunner he was." So the witnesses agree: the Baptist's words, spoken without a single sign of his own, are now seen to be wholly true, and the people, prepared by his testimony, recognize in Jesus the One he had pointed to — fruit the murderous city had refused (v.42).

The witnesses thus see the Baptist's witness vindicated at last: though 'John did no miracle,' all he spoke of Jesus proved true. Robertson's 'prepared soil for Christ' and Ellicott's key-note that 'the light is never absent' (John 1:5) together read the scene as the harvest of a faithful testimony, while Calvin marks the providence — Jerusalem's loss is the wilderness's gain — as the chapter moves to its closing note of many believing (v.42).

The people's verdict — that all John spoke was true — is thus the Evangelist's own, set down where the forerunner's testimony at last bears its fruit (John 1:34).
42:
"And many believed on him there." The chapter closes on a harvest of faith beyond Jordan, set against the rejection at Jerusalem.

The grammatical-historical readers mark the emphasis. Robertson: "and there many believed on him" — the same idiom as John 1:12, John 2:11 — "striking witness to the picture of the Messiah drawn by John. When Jesus came they recognized the original" (John 1:29-34). Ellicott: "there" stands "in a position of emphasis," marking "the contrast between the rejection in Jerusalem and the reception" beyond Jordan. Clarke: they believed on a twofold ground — "the testimony of John the Baptist," whom they knew to be a true prophet, and "the miracles which they saw Jesus work," which "proved that all John had said of him was true."

The Reformed voice (Gill, with Calvin's note at v.41) reads the providence: "this shows the reason of Christ's leaving Jerusalem and coming into these parts; there were others that were to believe in his name" (John 11:52).

So the witnesses agree: where the rulers reached for stones, the common people beyond Jordan believed — the Baptist's witness bearing fruit at last, and the Good Shepherd gathering his own outside the hostile city. The chapter that opened with thieves and robbers in the fold ends with true sheep hearing the Shepherd's voice and following him (v.27).

The witnesses thus close the chapter on a deliberate contrast: where the rulers reached for stones, the common people beyond Jordan believed. Robertson's note that 'they recognized the original' John had drawn (John 1:29-34), Ellicott's emphasis on the word 'there,' and Gill's that 'there were others that were to believe in his name' (John 11:52), together gather the Good Shepherd's own outside the hostile city — true sheep at last hearing his voice and following him (v.27).

So the discourse that opened with false shepherds in the fold ends with true sheep believing, the Shepherd gathering his own where the city's rage could not reach, a quiet harvest answering the long hostility of the chapter (v.16).
